questions.prompt: A team's origin was DDoS'd despite Cloudflare being in front. Investigation showed their mail subdomain pointed to the same IP as web. What was the cascade?

choices.text: Attackers `dig`ged the mail subdomain (gray-cloud, exposes real IP); got the origin IP; attacked it directly bypassing Cloudflare. Mitigations: split mail to a different IP, lock origin firewall to Cloudflare's IP ranges only (so even with leaks, attackers can't connect), audit for other leaks (CT logs, old DNS, etc.).

questions.explanation: Same-IP-for-mail-and-web is one of the most common origin IP leaks. Combined with no firewall lockdown, attackers can reliably bypass Cloudflare. Both mitigations matter: split IPs AND lock the firewall.

questions.prompt: Which Cloudflare API token policy is correct for CI/CD automation?

choices.text: Least-privilege per use case: separate tokens scoped to specific zones, with only the permissions each job needs (e.g. 'DNS edit on example.com' for DNS automation; 'Workers deploy on my-worker' for deploys). Rotate periodically; audit usage. Account-wide tokens turn a leak into a full compromise.

questions.explanation: Cloudflare's API tokens support fine-grained permissions. Use them. Each automation gets a token with only what it needs. Rotate quarterly. Audit token usage to catch unused/leaked tokens.
